label: short
chain_of_thought:
Okay, let's see. I need to find the concentration of salt when mixing two solutions. Hmm. So, there's 3 liters of a 20% salt solution and 7 liters of a 10% salt solution. I think the way to do this is to figure out how much salt is in each solution and then add them together, then divide by the total volume.

First, for the 3 liters of 20% solution. The amount of salt would be 20% of 3 liters. Let me calculate that. 20% is 0.2 in decimal, so 0.2 multiplied by 3. That gives 0.6 liters of salt.

Then, the 7 liters of 10% solution. 10% is 0.1, so 0.1 times 7. That's 0.7 liters of salt. Wait, hold on, 0.1 times 7 is 0.7? Wait, 10% of 7 is 0.7? Let me check. 10% of 10 is 1, so 10% of 7 is 0.7, yeah that's correct. Okay.

So total salt is 0.6 + 0.7, which is 1.3 liters. The total volume of the mixture is 3 + 7 = 10 liters. So the concentration is 1.3 liters salt divided by 10 liters total. Let me do that division. 1.3 divided by 10 is 0.13. To convert that to a percentage, multiply by 100, so 13%. 

Wait, that seems straightforward. Let me just make sure I didn't mix up any numbers. The first solution is 3 liters at 20%, so 0.6 liters. Second is 7 liters at 10%, which is 0.7. Total salt is 1.3 liters. Total volume 10 liters. 1.3/10 is 0.13, so 13%. Yeah, that seems right. I think that's the answer.
